article.title: Hatshepsut's Reign and the Politics of Memory in Egypt
article.html:
The Woman Who Would Be King: Hatshepsut's Reign and the Politics of Memory in Egypt

The Daily Brief — Ancient Egypt | North Africa

---

The Stone That Would Not Forget

Imagine a mason's chisel striking granite in the dead of night, working not to create but to erase. Imagine the careful, deliberate obliteration of a face — a royal face, a divine face — from a wall that had stood for twenty years. The nose gone. The cartouche, that oval ring of hieroglyphs that announced a pharaoh's name to gods and eternity alike, hammered into smooth, featureless stone. In some places, a replacement name was carved in its place. In others, the wall was simply left blank, an absence more conspicuous than any presence.

This is how one of the most remarkable reigns in three thousand years of Egyptian history was nearly lost to us. Not through war or plague or the slow dissolution of time, but through an act of deliberate, targeted forgetting. The woman who had ruled Egypt as pharaoh for roughly twenty years — who had sent trading fleets to distant Punt, who had raised two towering obelisks at Karnak that still partly stand today, who had built one of the most elegant mortuary temples in the ancient world — was systematically removed from the record.

Her name was Hatshepsut. And the story of what happened to her memory tells us as much about power as the story of her reign itself.

---

The World Before

To understand Hatshepsut, you have to understand what Egypt was, and what it expected.

By the time she came to power, around 1479 BCE, Egypt had already been a coherent civilization for roughly fifteen centuries. The pharaoh was not merely a king in the way we use that word today — a political head of state, a military commander, a lawgiver. The pharaoh was a living god, the earthly embodiment of Horus, and upon death became Osiris. The pharaoh was the hinge between the human world and the divine order, the figure whose proper performance of ritual and governance maintained ma'at — the cosmic balance of truth, justice, and harmony that kept the universe from collapsing into chaos.

This divine office had, in all the centuries since Narmer unified the Two Lands, been understood as male. The iconography was unambiguous: the double crown, the false beard, the crook and flail, the striding posture of authority — all of it was coded masculine. There had been women who exercised power in Egypt before Hatshepsut. Sobekneferu had ruled as pharaoh during the troubled end of the Middle Kingdom, around 1800 BCE, and there may have been others. But they were exceptional, transitional, and their reigns were brief. The institution of pharaoh was, in its bones, imagined as a son of Ra, a male heir in an unbroken patrilineal chain stretching back to the gods themselves.

Into this world came Hatshepsut, daughter of one of Egypt's most celebrated warrior-kings, Thutmose I, who had pushed Egypt's boundaries deep into Nubia and into the Levant. She grew up in the royal court at Thebes, educated, trained in ritual, surrounded by the machinery of empire. She was, by all evidence, formidably intelligent and politically astute. When her father died, she married her half-brother, who became Thutmose II. When Thutmose II died — after a short reign, probably in his early thirties — the throne passed to his son by a secondary wife, a boy named Thutmose III.

The boy was very young. Hatshepsut became regent.

---

The Reign Itself

What happened next is one of the most fascinating episodes in the ancient world, and also one of the most debated. Within a few years of acting as regent, Hatshepsut stopped being regent. She became pharaoh — not in name only, not in some carefully hedged feminine title, but pharaoh in the full, unambiguous, divine sense of the word.

She took the five-part royal titulary. She wore the double crown. She wore the false beard in official representations. She had herself depicted in statues as a man — broad-shouldered, striding, holding the crook and flail — even as inscriptions sometimes used feminine grammatical forms, as if the official record could not quite decide what she was. She took a throne name: Maatkare, Truth is the Soul of Ra. She was not hiding what she was doing. She was transforming what it meant.

Thutmose III did not disappear. He remained co-ruler, continued his military training, and eventually became one of Egypt's greatest generals. Some historians have imagined this as a story of usurpation, a scheming stepmother sidelining a legitimate heir. But the evidence does not really support that drama. There is no sign of conflict between them during her reign, and Thutmose III's own later career suggests he was not a man easily pushed around. The more plausible reading is that Hatshepsut and her court constructed a careful, theologically grounded justification for her rule, one that drew on her divine parentage and her demonstrated competence, and that Thutmose III — still a young man learning his craft — accepted this arrangement, at least for a time.

Her reign was, by any measure, a success. She was not a warrior-pharaoh in the mold of her father, though there is evidence of military campaigns into Nubia and possibly the Levant. What she built, instead, was a reputation as a builder and a trader. Her most celebrated achievement was the expedition to Punt — a distant land, probably on the coast of modern-day Somalia or Eritrea, that Egypt regarded as a semi-mythical place of origin, the land of the gods. The expedition returned with myrrh trees, ebony, ivory, gold, and live animals, all of it recorded in gorgeous painted relief on the walls of her mortuary temple at Deir el-Bahri. This was not just commerce. It was a theological statement: under Hatshepsut, Egypt was reconnected to the divine source.

Deir el-Bahri itself is perhaps her greatest monument. Nestled against the dramatic limestone cliffs on the west bank of the Nile at Thebes, the temple rises in three colonnaded terraces, its horizontal lines echoing the natural striations of the rock behind it. It is one of the most beautiful buildings to survive from the ancient world — serene, proportioned, at once monumental and somehow intimate. Her chief architect, Senenmut, is often credited with its design, and the relationship between Hatshepsut and Senenmut has been the subject of considerable speculation, including ancient graffiti of a sexually explicit nature found near the site. Whether they were lovers we cannot know. What we can say is that he was clearly one of the most powerful men in Egypt during her reign, holding an extraordinary accumulation of titles and offices.

She also raised two obelisks at Karnak, the great temple complex at Thebes. At roughly 29 meters tall and tipped with electrum — an alloy of gold and silver — they were designed to catch the first light of the sun each morning and blaze it across the city. One still stands. On its base, Hatshepsut wrote an inscription that has the quality of someone who knew she was doing something unusual and wanted to get her version of the story down for posterity: "Now my heart turns this way and that, as I think what the people will say — those who shall see my monuments in years to come."

She knew. She was thinking about memory even as she made it.

---

The Mystery: Who Erased Her, and Why?

Sometime after Hatshepsut's death — which occurred around 1458 BCE, when she would have been in her mid-forties — the erasure began. Her images were hacked from walls. Her statues were smashed and buried in a pit near her temple (where they were discovered in 1927 and painstakingly reassembled). Her name was removed from king lists. The campaign was thorough enough that for centuries after the rediscovery of ancient Egypt, scholars did not know she had existed, or misidentified the references to a female pharaoh as scribal errors.

The obvious suspect is Thutmose III. He outlived her and ruled for decades as sole pharaoh, eventually becoming the conqueror-king who created Egypt's greatest empire. But here is where the scholarly debate becomes genuinely interesting: the erasure did not happen immediately after her death. Egyptologists, through careful analysis of the construction phases at Karnak and other sites, now believe the erasure began perhaps twenty years after Hatshepsut died — toward the end of Thutmose III's own reign, or possibly even under his son, Amenhotep II.

This has profound implications for how we interpret the act. If it was revenge, why wait twenty years? The historian Egyptologist James Henry Breasted, working in the early twentieth century, was among those who cast Thutmose III as a resentful stepson nursing a decades-long grudge. But more recent scholars, including Egyptologist James P. Allen and the late Egyptologist Catharine Roehrig, have argued for a more nuanced reading. Perhaps the erasure was about succession — a concern that Hatshepsut's example might inspire future women to claim the throne, disrupting the orderly patrilineal succession that Thutmose III had worked to establish. Perhaps it was theological housekeeping, a tidying of the official record to remove an anomaly. Perhaps it was driven by Amenhotep II, who had his own reasons for wanting to consolidate a particular vision of dynastic legitimacy.

The honest answer is that we do not know. The ancient Egyptians left us no explanation. We have the chisel marks, the blank spaces, the pits of broken statuary, and we are left to infer motive from silence.

---

The Aftermath: What Rose From the Erasure

What is remarkable is that the erasure failed. Not completely — it succeeded well enough that Hatshepsut was lost for millennia, her identity confused and her achievements attributed to others. But the very thoroughness of the attempt to erase her has, paradoxically, preserved evidence of her everywhere. Every blank space on a wall is a document. Every pit of broken statues is an archive. The erasure required so much effort, touched so many monuments, that it became itself a kind of monument to the woman it was trying to unmake.

The rediscovery was gradual. Jean-François Champollion, the French scholar who deciphered hieroglyphics in the 1820s, noticed the anomalous feminine grammatical forms in royal inscriptions at Deir el-Bahri and was puzzled. Later in the nineteenth century, scholars began to piece together the picture. By the early twentieth century, the broad outlines were clear. The 1927 discovery of the statue pit at Deir el-Bahri, and the subsequent decades of painstaking work by the Metropolitan Museum of Art's Egyptian expedition, brought Hatshepsut's face back to the world.

Today, her reconstructed statues stand in the Metropolitan Museum of Art in New York. Her temple at Deir el-Bahri is one of the most visited archaeological sites in Egypt. The standing obelisk at Karnak still catches the morning light.

---

Why It Matters

Hatshepsut's story is not, at its heart, simply a story about a woman breaking barriers in the ancient world, though it is certainly that. It is a story about the relationship between power and memory — about who gets to write history, and what happens when the writers decide to erase.

Every era has its own version of this dynamic. The Soviet Union airbrushed disgraced officials from photographs. Statues of colonial figures are pulled down in city squares. Historical curricula are revised, revised again, contested. The impulse to control the past as a means of controlling the present is not a quirk of ancient tyranny. It is a recurring feature of political life wherever political life exists.

What Hatshepsut's story adds to this perennial drama is a note of stubborn irony: the erasure did not work. The blank spaces spoke. The broken statues survived in their pit. The feminine grammatical forms persisted in the stone that the chisels did not quite reach. History, it turns out, is not so easily unmade. The past has a way of reasserting itself through the very effort to suppress it — through the shape of the absence, through the overly insistent denial, through the archaeological record that no political will can fully anticipate or control.

She thought about what people would say, centuries later, when they looked at her monuments. She was right to think about it. We are still looking.

---

Further reading: Kara Cooney, The Woman Who Would Be King (2014); Egyptologist James P. Allen's contributions in Hatshepsut: From Queen to Pharaoh , ed. Catharine H. Roehrig (2005); and John Romer, A History of Ancient Egypt: From the First Farmers to the Great Pyramid (2012).

---

Tomorrow's read: The Indus Cities and the Mystery of Harappa.

---

locations.name: Deir el-Bahri
locations.caption: Site of Hatshepsut's mortuary temple, Djeser-Djeseru — one of the finest buildings of the ancient world, and where her smashed statues were buried and later recovered.

locations.name: Karnak Temple Complex, Thebes
locations.caption: Home of Hatshepsut's two great obelisks, including the one still standing today, tipped in electrum to catch the rising sun.

locations.name: Thebes (Luxor)
locations.caption: Capital of Egypt during the New Kingdom and center of Hatshepsut's reign, seat of the royal court and the god Amun.

locations.name: Memphis
locations.caption: Ancient administrative capital of Egypt, a key node of power and trade throughout the pharaonic era.

locations.name: Land of Punt (approx. Horn of Africa)
locations.caption: The semi-legendary trading destination of Hatshepsut's celebrated expedition, returning with myrrh trees, gold, ivory, and live animals — recorded in relief at Deir el-Bahri.

locations.name: Nubia (Kerma region)
locations.caption: Region to the south of Egypt where Hatshepsut conducted military campaigns and from which Egypt extracted gold and other resources.

locations.name: Valley of the Kings
locations.caption: Royal necropolis on the west bank at Thebes where Hatshepsut was buried — her mummy now identified in the Cairo Museum through DNA and CT analysis.

markets.title: US Markets Today
bullets: 📈 FUTURES POINTING HIGHER: S&P 500 futures +0.6% pre-open as US-Iran peace deal signals strengthen — Trump says a formal agreement could be signed in Switzerland as soon as Sunday, with Iran's state media confirming a draft is nearly finalized.

📅 YESTERDAY'S CLOSE: Dow +1.86% (+930 pts to 50,849), S&P 500 +1.75% to 7,394, Nasdaq +2.54% — all three snapped a two-day losing streak on Trump's Iran ceasefire pivot and a chipmaker rebound of nearly 8%.

📊 TODAY'S DATA: University of Michigan preliminary Consumer Sentiment (June) at 9:00 AM CT — consensus expects a bounce to 46 from 44.8, still near record lows; embedded inflation expectations are the key number to watch ahead of next week's FOMC.

🚀 SPACEX IPO: SpaceX (SPCX) begins trading on Nasdaq today, priced at $135/share, raising ~$75B — the largest IPO in history, implying a ~$1.78T valuation; index inclusion mechanics and passive-fund forced buying are live debates.

🌍 OVERNIGHT GLOBAL MARKETS: Asia and Europe broadly higher on Iran deal optimism — India's Nifty 50 +1.6%, Sensex +1.9%; European DAX, FTSE, and CAC all closed Thursday with modest gains of ~0.5–0.6%.

🛢️ OIL & RATES MOVING: Crude oil -2% to ~$85/bbl on peace deal hopes, pulling back from recent highs; 10-yr Treasury yield eased to ~4.47% after dropping ~10 bps Thursday — relief from the Iran energy shock, but May PPI came in at +6.5% YoY (3-year high), keeping rate-hike bets alive.

⚠️ THEME TO WATCH — IRAN DEAL vs. FOMC SETUP: Markets are pricing a best-case weekend peace signing, but no final text is approved yet; conflicting reports could whipsaw equities intraday. The FOMC meeting begins Monday (June 16), with a rate decision Wednesday — markets currently price no cut in 2026 and rising odds of a hike.

trivia.question: Which golfer won the 2023 PGA Championship at Oak Hill Country Club, claiming his third major title and first since 2019?
trivia.story: Collin Morikawa captured his third major championship at the 2023 PGA Championship with a dominant performance at Oak Hill, finishing at 10-under par. The victory marked a significant resurgence for Morikawa, whose previous major win came at the 2021 Open Championship, and established him as one of golf's rising elite competitors alongside other young major winners.
